title: Technological Changes and Countries’ Tax Policy Design: Evidence from Anti–Tax Avoidance Rules
abstract: We investigate the association between technological changes and corporate tax policies in 34 OECD countries between 1996 and 2016. We use a shift-share design to capture the differential exposure of countries to U.S. technological advancements. Our study shows that countries’ antitax avoidance rules are tightened as their exposure to U.S. technological advancements increases. The tightening is particularly concentrated in countries that are larger, more exposed to intangibles, and have higher profit shifting incentives. Our findings have important implications for corporate executives as a country’s anti–tax avoidance rules are associated with foreign technological advancements.

author.Martin Jacob: Department of Accounting and Control, IESE Business School, 08034 Barcelona, Spain
author.Harm H. Schütt: Department of Accountancy, Tilburg School of Economics and Management, 5037 AB Tilburg, Netherlands

article.1. Introduction: A large literature in accounting examines how firms’ tax avoidance activities respond to tax rules. Examples are profit shifting responses to tax rates (Hines and Rice 1994,Huizinga and Laeven 2008,Klassen and Laplante 2012,De Simone et al. 2017) or to anti–tax avoidance rules (Buettner et al. 2012,Alexander et al. 2020,Joshi 2020). To fully understand how anti–tax avoidance rules shape firm outcomes, it is important to also understand which potential factors are associated with regulation (Hail et al. 2018). For example, the documented evidence on firms’ tax avoidance in response to anti–tax avoidance rules might depend on the tax regulation in place during the sample period in the respective country. Tax rules change frequently, and these changes might be associated with economic forces, such as economic development or technological change, which also influence firm behavior of interest to researchers. Thus, academics and policymakers require an understanding of which forces are associated with the adoption of certain tax rules. Understanding when and how policymakers adjust corporate tax regulation is important also for managers whose strategic decisions are often based on assumptions and expectations about forward-looking effective marginal tax rates (Scholes et al. 2014,Graham et al. 2017). This paper analyzes whether technological change, a key driver of firm behavior, is associated with the subsequent adoption of anti–tax avoidance rules, which gained substantial popularity over the past decades (OECD 2015,2018). Technology, broadly defined, is what combines inputs (e.g., employees, machinery, or equipment) to produce output. Technological changes then are commonly understood as changes in how efficiently inputs are turned into output, that is, as changes in productivity. These productivity boosts are due to changes in factor input and improvements of how effectively these inputs result in output, for example, through the rising importance of information technology, automation, or robots. Conceptually, two forms of technological change are particularly relevant to our analysis. First, technological change can facilitate and trigger the increased use of intangibles (Syverson 2011,Jones 2016). Second, technological change implies that firms become more mobile (Acemoglu and Restrepo 2019,Hallward-Driemeier and Nayyar 2019,Stapleton and Webb 2022).1 Importantly, these two consequences of technological changes create new avenues for tax avoidance and profit shifting (Grubert 2003,Dischinger and Riedel 2011). Technological changes can thus produce a mismatch between existing tax rules and new economic realities (Desai and Hines 2004). We thus predict that there is a positive association between technological change and the subsequent adoption of anti–tax avoidance rules to limit the increased profit shifting opportunities arising from technological change (i.e., greater use of intangibles and more firm mobility). However, because policymakers could also adjust other tax rules (e.g., tax rates), we might not observe an association between technological change and anti–tax avoidance rules. It is thus an empirical question of whether technological change is associated with a subsequent tightening of anti–tax avoidance rules. To test this prediction, we use a sample of 34 member countries of the Organisation for Economic Cooperation and Development (OECD) over the period 1996–2016 for which we collect tax policy information. We construct an index consisting of nine items capturing rules that combat tax avoidance (anti–tax avoidance rules).2Among others, these nine items comprise thin-capitalization rules, transfer pricing regimes, controlled foreign company rules, and disclosure regimes designed to reduce cross-border profit shifting (Buettner et al. 2012,Dharmapala 2014,Alexander et al. 2020,Joshi 2020).3 We face two main empirical challenges to test the association between technological change and tax rules. First, because a country’s corporate tax policy might spur (or hinder) technological changes in the country, a country’s technological change is potentially endogenous with respect to its own tax rules. Second, measuring technological change is inherently challenging because data on inputs to technological change (e.g., automation or robots) are hard to measure or unavailable. We try to overcome these two challenges by (1) using a shift-share design (Bartik 1991,Breuer 2022) and (2) deriving an output-based measure of technological change using United States industry-level data. Specifically, our research design explores differences in countries’ exposure to common U.S. technological changes. This exposure varies across countries and over time because (1) countries have different exposure to certain industries and (2) industries experience technological change at different points in time. Our rationale for using U.S. technological changes is that given the role of the U.S. in the global economy, U.S. technological changes are likely to be correlated with global technological changes.4Our measure of U.S. technological change is based on the notion of how efficiently input factors (e.g., employees, machinery, or equipment) are turned into output. Put differently, we use an output-based measure of productivity changes because this is a clearer indicator that some form of change has occurred. We use data from the multifactor productivity database of the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, which provides industry-level information for the United States. Our resulting measure of technological change is a common factor of changes in (1) total factor productivity, (2) capital intensity, and (3) labor productivity.5 The combination of our tax policy proxies and the measure of exposure to U.S. technological changes allows us to examine how foreign technological changes are associated with anti–tax avoidance rules over the period 1996–2016. We first present descriptive evidence on our main tax policy measure. We observe a trend toward stricter anti–tax avoidance rules. However, in contrast to harmonization efforts by policymakers and the OECD, the variation across countries in anti–tax avoidance rules remained high and even increased slightly over time. We also observe substantial variation in exposure to U.S. technological change across countries. Korea, Japan, Finland, and Israel are among the countries with the greatest exposure to U.S. technological changes, whereas Norway, New Zealand, and Iceland are less exposed to U.S. technological changes. The latter is mainly due to the industry composition (e.g., the importance of the oil industry in Norway versus the importance of electronic products in Korea, Finland, and Japan). In our main empirical analysis, we test whether past exposure to U.S. technological changes are associated with subsequent anti–tax avoidance rules. We combine a lead–lag design followingReinhart and Rogoff (2011)andHail et al. (2018)with a shift-share design to establish the association between foreign technological change and tax rules. We control for a battery of country-level controls, country fixed effects (to account for time-invariant country characteristics associated with exposure to U.S. technological change and with anti–tax avoidance rules), and year fixed effects (to account for general global economic, technological, and tax policy trends). We find robust evidence that exposure to U.S. technological change is associated with stricter anti–tax avoidance rules. Our main finding of a positive association between exposure to U.S. technological change and anti–tax avoidance rules is robust to various robustness tests, for example, to the exclusion of single industries from the measure of exposure to U.S. technological change or from altering other assumptions of our empirical approach.6 We also assess whether our results are related toreactivetax policy (i.e., policymakers react to changes in tax avoidance but not to technological changes) or if our results also captureproactivetax policy (i.e., the positive association between exposure to U.S. technological change and anti–tax avoidance rules also reflects that policymakers anticipate potential increases in profit shifting). We take two steps to show that at least some part of the observed association is due to proactive tax policy. First, we control for reactive tax policy through additional controls that capture the tax avoidance behavior of firms at the aggregate level (Shevlin et al. 2019) and profit shifting incentives (Huizinga and Laeven 2008). Second, we explore differences in countries’ long-term orientation. We find that more long-term–oriented countries, that is, those countries that likely also act more proactively, exhibit a stronger positive association between exposure to U.S. technological change and anti–tax avoidance rules. These results are consistent with at least part of the policy response being proactive. In the final step, we assess the two channels—greater use of intangibles and greater firm mobility—through which exposure to U.S. technological change might be associated with anti–tax avoidance rules. First, we assess the role of intangible assets by exploring differences in a country’s exposure to U.S. knowledge stock. The idea is that if there is a stronger (weaker) tie of a country to the U.S. knowledge stock, that is, to intangibles, then this should strengthen (weaken) the association of technological change and anti–tax avoidance rules. Using the measure of knowledge stock byVenturini (2015), we find evidence consistent with this prediction. We corroborate this finding using a measure of tax incentives to shift profits, for example, via intangibles. We find that if profit shifting incentives are high (low), there is a strong (weak) positive association between technological change and anti–tax avoidance rules. Second, we explore the role of greater firm mobility. The latter induces the risk of firms leaving the country (“capital flight”). However, not all countries are similarly exposed to this risk. To test for differences in this risk, we use the notion of agglomeration rents, where higher agglomeration rents imply that capital is less mobile (Baldwin and Krugman 2004). We argue that there are higher agglomeration rents in larger economies because local markets are bigger and there are more economic activities in closer vicinity. We use country size to proxy for agglomeration rents and capital mobility, with larger countries being at a lower risk of capital flight than smaller countries. Thus, in contrast to smaller countries, larger countries can tighten antitax avoidance rules, as they face less risk of firms leaving the country. We find evidence consistent with the notion that exposure to U.S. technological change is associated with stricter anti–tax avoidance rules when countries are at lower risk of capital flight due to agglomeration rents, that is, in larger countries. We find support for this notion by splitting the sample based on trade openness. We show a significant (insignificant) association between exposure to U.S. technological change and anti–tax avoidance rules for low (high) levels of trade openness. The intuition is that countries with lower (higher) trade openness have less (more) elastic capital flows, that is, face a lower (higher) risk of capital flight (Horst 1971). Our study contributes to the literature in two main ways. First, we contribute to the literature on the determinants and consequences of tax policy.Vegh and Vuletin (2015)explore the role of cyclicality in setting tax rates, andClausing (2007)examines tax revenue consequences. We show how exposure to U.S. technological changes is associated with tighter anti–tax avoidance rules, which is also a likely determinant of various firm actions of interest to accounting scholars, such as investments and tax avoidance. Our findings are consistent with countries adjusting their anti–tax avoidance rules to meet the new economic realities induced by technological changes. We thus complement prior research by showing that technological changes are associated with corporate tax rules and, in particular, with tighter anti–tax avoidance rules. As taxes are a key part of the regulatory framework within which firms operate, it is important to understand which variables are associated with future regulatory changes. Second, we contribute to the literature on the tax policy design and the “race to the bottom” of corporate tax rates (Kanbur and Keen 1993,Kind et al. 2000,Devereux et al. 2008,Hong and Smart 2010,Devereux 2012). Prior literature suggests that countries compete by lowering their statutory tax rates (Slemrod 2004,Devereux et al. 2008). Our results add to this academic but also political debate by highlighting the role of anti–tax avoidance rules. Although exposure to U.S. technological change is associated with tighter anti–tax avoidance rules, there is heterogeneity across countries. For example, there is no association for smaller countries. Possibly by abstaining from tighter anti–tax avoidance rules, smaller countries create indirect investment incentives, whereas larger countries with high agglomeration rents can use stricter anti–tax avoidance rules to preserve tax revenues. Thus, despite multilateral harmonization efforts—such as the OECD Base Erosion and Profit Shifting (BEPS) project—countries might set tax policies in a way that best caters to their unique national interests.7Hence, international harmonization efforts such as the OECD BEPS project may not curb tax competition among countries. Finally, we believe that the measures we develop in our study and that we make available to researchers—the tax policy measures and the cross-country exposure to U.S. technological change data—may be valuable for future empirical research on the real effects of taxation or the consequences of technological changes. Our tax policy variables provide a comprehensive overview of different tax policy tools used in OECD countries over two decades. Our technological change measure of exposure to U.S. technological change could be a useful resource in cross-country settings. We acknowledge that our study faces several limitations. Our measure of exposure to U.S. technological changes, despite being rooted in economic theory and derived from the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, can measure such changes only on a coarse level. Similarly, our index capturing anti–tax avoidance rules is summary measures with some arguably arbitrary choices, such as the weighting of each item similarly. Another potential limitation is that we can only examine the rules set by policymakers and not the level of enforcement of those rules. Finally, our approach does not enable us to derive causal relations. We can only assess associations with our approach.
article.2. Hypothesis Development: 2.1. Defining Technological Change Defining technological change is inherently difficult due to the broad nature of this term. We build our definition on literature on development economics, macroeconomics, and industrial organization, where there is “widespread consensus that technological differences are a central determinant of productivity differences across firms, regions, and nations” (Acemoglu 2010, p. 1037). Productivity is about efficiency, that is, how much output can be produced with a given set of inputs (Syverson 2011). Technology, broadly defined, is what combines inputs (e.g., employees, machinery, or equipment) to produce output. Technological changes then are commonly understood as changes in how efficiently inputs are turned into output. In other words, we understand technological change broadly as changes in productivity and changes in factor input (e.g., the rising importance of information technology) driving the productivity boost. Consistent with this view, the literature finds information and communication technology (ICT), the stock of technological knowledge in the economy, and the spread of automation processes to be technological factors strongly associated with increased productivity (Venturini 2015,Acemoglu and Restrepo 2019). We understand this increased productivity as technological change.8 Conceptually, two forms of technological change are particularly relevant for our analysis. First, technological change can trigger changes in total factor productivity (TFP) (Solow 1957,Romer 1990,Syverson 2011,Jones 2016,Bloom et al. 2019). The canonical example for such an increase in TFP is knowledge creation by public or private R&D activity, that is, through the creation of intangible assets (Kortum 1997,Jones 2016). The second form is technological change that affects the relative productivity and thus importance of factor inputs, labor versus capital (Acemoglu 2002). A prominent example is the increased labor productivity driven by technological advancements that also facilitates greater offshoring activities.Acemoglu and Restrepo (2019)provide several additional historical examples of technological advancements (e.g., the mechanization of agriculture or the rise of industrial robots and other automated machinery) that have increased productivity and resulted in new tasks or jobs being created. Prior literature also shows that the emergence of ICT has resulted in productivity gains for labor input (Graetz and Michaels 2018), but that the productivity effects of technology vary across industries (Morrison 1997). Because such new jobs, for example, programming or app development, are more mobile, technological advancements contribute to firms’ mobility. In sum, technological changes can have several potential consequences, which we subsume under two categories that are relevant for anti–tax avoidance rules. First, technological change can facilitate and trigger the increased use of intangibles (Kortum 1997,Venturini 2015,Jones 2016). Second, technological change (e.g., ICT, automatization, and robot-induced labor productivity gains) facilitates the mobility of firms and offshoring activities (Hallward-Driemeier and Nayyar 2019,Stapleton and Webb 2022). We next discuss how technological changes, through greater use of intangibles and greater firm mobility, are potentially associated with anti–tax avoidance rules. 2.2. Technological Change and Anti–Tax Avoidance Rules To derive our main hypothesis, it is important to first consider the policymakers’ rationale for setting corporate tax policies. Policymakers balance multiple potentially conflicting objectives, such as attracting investment in a competitive global market for capital and labor (Kind et al. 2000), combating tax avoidance to obtain an equitable tax system wherein every firm pays its “fair” share, and securing predictable, less procyclical corporate tax revenues to enable government spending (Goncharov and Jacob 2014). Technological changes represent a shock to the equilibrium in which countries have set their tax policy. This is because technological changes can create new opportunities for tax avoidance and can facilitate profit shifting not only through the greater use of intangible assets but also through greater firm mobility (Drake et al. 2020). Thus, after large technological changes, anti–tax avoidance rules may have to be changed to keep pace with new economic realities (Desai and Hines 2004).9As corporate attempts to exploit legal arrangements to reduce tax payments can be constrained by rules such as thin-capitalization rules, transfer pricing documentation requirements, or controlled foreign company rules (Buettner et al. 2012,Lohse and Riedel 2013,Heckemeyer and Overesch 2017,Alexander et al. 2020), technological changes are potentially associated with countries introducing or tightening anti–tax avoidance rules. We thus formulate our main hypothesis as follows. Technological changes are associated with tighter anti–tax avoidance rules. This hypothesis is not without tension because there are reasons why there might not be an association between technological change and tighter antitax avoidance rules. Specifically, as countries compete for firms, capital, and talents, they might not want to deter firms by tightening anti–tax avoidance rules. Instead, technological change might be associated with changes in other corporate tax policy tools. For example, although countries could reduce statutory tax rates to attract investments and mobile firms, they could also set investment incentives via tax base elements (e.g., immediate write-off rules for selected assets) or specific tools to attract intangible assets (e.g., patent boxes).10Our empirical approach will account for such potential alternative policy tools. In supplemental tests, we will also explore whether and how technological change is associated with changes in corporate tax rates or investment tax incentives.
article.3. Data Collection and Research Design: 3.1. Sample Construction Our sample comprises data from 34 OECD countries over the period 1996–2016. We focus on OECD countries for several reasons. First, data on industry weights, which are necessary to construct our measure of a country’s exposure to U.S. technological changes, are available only for OECD countries. Moreover, we can collect detailed data on tax policy characteristics for these countries. Second, the extent of economic integration of most OECD countries allows us to focus on tax policy changes at the country level while accounting for competitive pressures and policy externalities among countries. Taking all OECD countries as a starting point, we drop Estonia from our sample because it does not levy any corporate income tax as long as profits are retained. We further drop the United States to overcome endogeneity concerns, as our core explanatory variable is the exposure to U.S. technological changes. Another research design choice that requires explanation relates to our sample period that starts in 1996. Prior to our sample period, international trade and foreign direct investment (FDI) were at very low levels. With the removal of trade barriers and the fall of the Iron Curtain, FDI started to increase rapidly in the 1990s. Thus, the necessity to implement anti–tax avoidance rules emerged given the race-to-the bottom in corporate tax rates. In other words, before the mid-1990s, tax policy was likely shaped by different concerns compared with our sample period, where cross-border profit shifting and mobile capital have assumed a major role in the globalized world. 3.2. Measure Construction and Variation 3.2.1. Measuring Anti–Tax Avoidance Rules. The main focus of the empirical analysis is on anti–tax avoidance rules.11Because countries can use a wide range of rules to combat profit shifting and tax avoidance, we construct an indexAntiTAScorethat comprises a country’s set of anti–tax avoidance rules. As listed inTable 1,AntiTAScoremeasures countries’ corporate tax rules for combating corporate tax avoidance based on nine items. Most of these items are also specific actions in the OECD BEPS initiative. However, not all countries have implemented these suggestions at the same time or at all, as these are nonbinding recommendations. We use the following nine items: To start, we include binary items for (1) the existence of a general antiavoidance rule, (2) hybrid mismatch rules, (3) an exit tax regime, (4) a tax-related disclosure regime, and (5) country-by-country reporting rules. Moreover, (6) a categorical variable accounting for the progressiveness of the transfer pricing regime is included inAntiTAScore. To account for (7) the strictness of controlled foreign corporation (CFC) regimes, we construct a measure that consists of the low-tax threshold set in a CFC regime divided by the statutory tax rate in the country of control. Larger values of our CFC item thus represent stricter CFC regimes. We also account for the strictness of (8) earnings stripping and (9) thin-capitalization rules in a country, building on the methodology ofBuettner et al. (2012). The choice of these items is based on prior literature, which has shown that thin-capitalization rules, transfer pricing documentation requirements, country-by-country reporting, or CFC rules are able to curb profit shifting (Buettner et al. 2012,Lohse and Riedel 2013,Heckemeyer and Overesch 2017,Alexander et al. 2020,Joshi 2020). Higher values ofAntiTAScoreindicate greater regulatory effort to combat corporate tax avoidance. We cross-check country-year observations of specific index items with the data ofAlexander et al. (2020)andJacob et al. (2019)as long as the same country-years and items are available. 3.2.2. Trends in Anti–Tax Avoidance Rules. Figure 1presents the average time trends for anti–tax avoidance rules. We observe a trend toward stricter anti–tax avoidance rules. Countries seem to implement anti–tax avoidance rules toward the end of the sample period. This observation could be attributable to the OECD BEPS initiative that began in 2012. Moreover, many countries had recently implemented detailed transfer pricing documentation requirements to counteract firms’ primary profit shifting strategies (Lohse and Riedel 2013,Heckemeyer and Overesch 2017).Figure 1also reports the variation of these tax rules across countries. The gray areas depict interquartile ranges, revealing substantial variation in the general trend. Interestingly, we do not observe any convergence in anti–tax avoidance rules across countries during our sample period. Instead, variation in countries’ tax policy seems to increase in the latter part of our sample despite harmonization efforts in anti–tax avoidance legislation in the European Union and the OECD. Figure A.2 in the Online Appendix presents the trend in anti–tax avoidance rules (AntiTAScore) for each sample country. Although some countries implemented a variety of anti–tax avoidance rules (e.g., Australia, Canada, Germany, and France), others exhibited rather lowAntiTAScorelevels (e.g., Switzerland, Korea, and Slovenia). In addition, the timing of the implementation of anti–tax avoidance rules varies across countries. Some countries, such as the Netherlands and France, implemented anti–tax avoidance rules early on, whereas other countries, such as Chile and Ireland, only recently enacted such rules. Australia and the United Kingdom can be regarded as pioneers in the implementation of certain anti–tax avoidance practices, which is reflected in their highAntiTAScorelevels and their recent policy actions.12Taken together,Figure 1and Figure A.2 suggest that changes in anti–tax avoidance rules vary over time and across countries and do not cluster around single years or single countries. 3.2.3. Measuring Exposure to U.S. Technological Change. As country-specific technological changes are potentially endogenous to a country’s tax policy and as the necessary data to derive country-specific technological changes are available only for few OECD countries, we use a measure of technological change based on U.S. data and focus on the relation between a country’s tax policy dimensions and itsexposureto U.S. technological changes. We use exposure to U.S. technological changes (labeledTechExposure) as our main independent variable of interest—rather than focusing on country-specific technological changes directly—for two reasons. First, U.S. technological changes are plausibly exogenous to any of the other OECD countries’ tax policy changes (which we aim to validate in Table A.1 in the Online Appendix). Second, research on knowledge spillovers documents significant spillovers from the United States. For example,Eaton and Kortum (1999)find that at least one-third of productivity growth, even in other high-R&D countries, such as the United Kingdom, Japan, France, and Germany, is due to research in the United States. Thus, U.S. technological changes are a likely source of technological changes in other countries. The construction ofTechExposurefollows three steps. First, we derive three variables capturing U.S. technological change using U.S. industry-level data. Second, we turn these into country–year specific exposure proxies by weighting each industry measure by how important that industry is for the focal country. Third, we extract the largest common factor of the three exposure measures via factor analysis. We use this common factor as our measure of U.S. technological change exposure. We start by explaining how we measure U.S. technological change. Measuring technological change is inherently difficult, as it is not directly observable. Although the literature has documented many antecedents, such as R&D, the timing between those inputs and an eventual technological change is often unclear. For example, increasing R&D is not guaranteed to lead to innovation, and if it does, the timing varies substantially. Changes in productivity are a clearer indicator that some form of change has occurred. We thus use different components of industry-level productivity changes that can be linked to technological changes. Conceptually, we start with a classic Cobb-Douglas production function that expresses outputQas a function of capitalK, laborL, and a term called TFP (denotedA), which is also called “stock of knowledge” (Romer 1990):𝑄𝑡=𝐴𝑡⁢𝐾𝛼𝑡⁢𝐿𝛽𝑡Qt=At⁢Ktα⁢Ltβ. As discussed inSection 2.1, two forms of technological change are relevant for our analysis. The first leads to change in the total productivity of all factors, thus increasingA. The second form changes the relative productivity of the factors (the ratio ofαtoβ). Capturing both forms is complicated by interdependencies and measurement issues. For example, higher labor productivity, a comparably easy-to-measure proxy (Q/L), can be due to increases inAorβbut also changes in capital intensity (K/L). Capital intensity in turn should also be related to relative factor productivity (e.g., if labor becomes more productive, factor prices change, and the economy will substitute labor for capital accordingly). For this reason, we take three U.S. industry–level productivity growth measures (i.e., U.S. changes in TFP, changes in labor productivity, and contribution of capital intensity to labor productivity) that are correlated with both forms of technological change (greater use of intangibles and greater firm mobility). To turn them into exposure measures, we weight these three measures with weights according to how important the respective industry is for the focal country of interest. Finally, we use factor analysis to extract the largest common factor of all three weighted measures. Its factor score constitutes our measure of exposure to U.S. technological change (TechExposure). Data for all three U.S. industry–level productivity growth measures come from the multifactor productivity database of the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics (www.bls.gov/mfp). The database contains various industry-level time series related to labor productivity and has the advantage of being available on a detailed sector basis and over a long time series. In line with the productivity literature, we focus on manufacturing and service industries and drop sectors such as agriculture, mining, utilities, and government services from these data (Bartelsman et al. 2013). From the BLS Multifactor Productivity database, we extract yearly changes in TFP (USΔTFP), yearly changes in the contribution of capital intensity to productivity (USΔCapInt), and yearly changes in labor productivity (USΔLabProd) per industry.13Moreover,USΔTFPcaptures technological change that changes the production efficiency of all inputs equally, such as certain advances in information technology, andUSΔCapIntcaptures changes in labor productivity that derive from more or less efficient use of capital goods. In particular, large changes in capital intensity are often attributed to technological change (e.g., the advent of lean production facilities (Van Biesebroeck 2003)). The third variable,USΔLabProd, subsumes the other two measures, reflecting overall changes in labor efficiency. It is our catchall measure for all sources of productivity changes, including what might not be captured in the other two owing to measurement error. In the second step, we then construct our country–year-specific measures of these three components. That is, we measure the exposure of a country to these changes. From the three measuresUSΔTFP,USΔCapInt, andUSΔLabProd, we construct three time-varying country-level exposure variables (ΔTFP, ΔLabProd, and ΔCapInt) as weighted averages. We use industry weights from 1995 for industryjas our weights (𝐼𝑛𝑑𝑢𝑠𝑡𝑟𝑦𝑉𝐴𝑑𝑑𝑖,𝑗,𝑡=1995IndustryVAddi,j,t=1995). For example, ΔTFPfor countryiin periodtis defined as follows: The resulting ΔTFP, ΔLabProd, and ΔCapIntmeasure countryi’s exposure to the different facets of technological change in the United States. We consider a country to be more exposed to technological changes in an U.S. industry if that industryjis more important for the focal countryi. We measure importance via the industry’s share of value creation inside the country. The rationale is that an industry with a large share of value creation is (1) more likely to be exposed to U.S. technological changes and (2) more relevant for tax policy setting.14We use OECD industry-level input–output data to construct these proportion weights. For each OECD country and year, we then weight each U.S. industry-year observation ofUSΔTFP,USΔLabProd, andUSΔCapIntby the industry’s importance for the country of interest and compute a weighted average to arrive at ΔTFP, ΔLabProd, and ΔCapInt, respectively. An important part of our identification strategy is that we construct the industry weights using value added data from the beginning of our sample period (1995). Thus, we apply a shift-share design similar to Bartik weights (Bartik 1991,Breuer 2022).15Although this eliminates variation in these weights, the advantage is that the resulting predetermined weights—by construction—cannot be endogenous to local (i.e., country-specific) tax policy changes (addressing reverse causality), local technological changes, local economic growth, or other country-specific economic changes during our sample period (after 1995). Thus, the only variation over time in our measures comes from the variation in U.S. technological changes. The variation across countries stems from variation in the predetermined weights across countries. In the final step, we combine these three variables into one technological change measure. As explained above, all three proxies, ΔTFP, ΔLabProd, and ΔCapInt, capture different aspects of exposure to U.S. technological change with varying degrees of measurement error. The correlations inTable 2, Panel B, suggest that labor productivity and TFP have a significant commonality, whereas capital intensity seems more distinct, at least based on univariate correlations. We standardize all three proxies and combine them into one measure using factor analysis.16Based on the assumption that all three measures are determined by an underlying latent technological change factor, we extract the scores of the first factor. We denote itTechExposureand use it as the main measure of the exposure to U.S. technological change of a country. We also show results using the three original proxies separately. The lower triangle shows Spearman correlation coefficients. SeeAppendix Afor a detailed description of the definition and computation of each variable. 3.2.4. Trends in Technological Change. Figure 2plots technological change by U.S. industry showing how U.S. technological change varies by industry and over time. AsTechExposureis a standardized factor with a mean of zero to ease the interpretation, values can be negative. Thus, a negative value forTechExposuredoes not imply technological regress. Consistent with our expectations, most technological changes occur in the computer and electronic products industry. The motor vehicle industry and the information communication industry also show regular technological progress. In the next step, we combine the exposure to U.S. technological changes (TechExposure) and anti–tax tax avoidance rules.Figure 3plots the average value ofTechExposurefor each country over the sample period on thexaxis. On theyaxis, we plot the anti–tax avoidance score in 2017. A few descriptive statistics are noteworthy. The top fiveTechExposurecountries are Korea, Finland, Ireland, Japan, and Israel. These are the countries whose historically (i.e., in 1995) most important industries experienced the highest average amount of technological changes in the United States, such as the computer and electronic equipment industry. Moreover, there appears to be a positive association between the strength of anti–tax avoidance rules in 2017 and exposure to U.S. technological change over the sample period. This is consistent with ourHypothesis 1that countries with greater exposure to U.S. technological change develop stricter antitax avoidance rules.17 3.2.5. Measuring Other Corporate Tax Policy Tools. In addition to anti–tax avoidance rules, we also collect information on statutory tax rates, denotedStatTR. We collect data on statutory corporate tax rates from the OECD Stats database and the Ernst and YoungCorporate Tax Guides.18Figure A.1 in the Online Appendix reports the trend in tax rates during our sample period. There is a decline in the tax rates, but still a high variation across countries. The correlations inTable 2further show that tax rates are correlated with anti–tax avoidance rules, but the correlation is rather modest. Moreover, countries can change the definition of the tax base to stimulate investment in a country. We combine these incentives in an index (InvScore). As listed in Table A.2 in the Online Appendix,InvScorecomprises seven components, including tax loss carryback rules, tax loss carryforward rules, R&D tax incentives, allowance for corporate equity, group tax relief, tax rules granting beneficial tax treatment to venture capital investments, and depreciation rules.19Figure A.1 in the Online Appendix reports the trend in investment incentives during our sample period. There is a slight increase in investment incentives, but as for anti–tax avoidance rules, there is a high variation across countries. 3.3. Research Design and Descriptive Statistics Our research design follows the approach ofReinhart and Rogoff (2011)andHail et al. (2018)and combines it with a shift-share design (Bartik 1991,Breuer 2022). We model the time series of anti–tax avoidance rules as functions of their own past value, the other corporate tax policy variables (also lagged), and the past values of exposure to U.S. technological changes. Our main specification is as follows: The vector𝒙𝒊,𝒕−𝟏xi,t−1consists of several controls for macroeconomic and political conditions, which follow prior literature (Devereux et al. 2008). They are obtained from the OECD and the World Bank. We include lagged values of government expenditures scaled by gross domestic product (GDP) (GovExp), GDP growth (GDPGr), log GDP per capita (lnGDPpCap), and log GDP (lnGDP). We measure unemployment as a percentage of the total labor force in a country (Unemp). We include the following two controls for the openness of a country: capital flow openness, measured as the sum of the stock of inflowing and outflowing FDI scaled by GDP (FDIStock), and trade openness, measured as exports plus imports divided by GDP (TradeOpen). We include two indicator variables for political influences: whether a year is an election year (ElYear) and whether the ruling party in a country is considered leaning toward state control rather than free markets (ProState). We compute both variables based on data from the ParlGov Initiative (Döring et al. 2022). Finally, we include the first principal component of a country’s World Bank governance indicators as a control for that country’s overall governance quality (GovIndex).Appendix Alists detailed variable descriptions. Given that our main independent variable is the exposure to U.S. technological change, our fixed effects structure requires some explanation. We include country fixed effects (αi) to account for time-invariant country level factors that can drive both, exposure to U.S. technological change and anti–tax avoidance rules. We thus explore deviations from the average country-specific exposure to U.S. technological change.22We also include year fixed effects (αt). AsHail et al. (2018)note, the inclusion of year fixed effects in this setup is very conservative because they capture general trends, including those related to our variables of interest. However, given the nature of ourTechExposuremeasure as being the exposure to U.S. technological change, it is important to account for the general time trends. We cluster standard errors at the country level. Our research design inEquation (2)offers the advantage of reducing collinearity through the inclusion of a single three-period backward-looking moving average instead of multiple lags (Reinhart and Rogoff 2011). Accounting for more than one lag is crucial because it is ex ante unclear whether tax policy is associated with large technological changes and, if so, when. Thus, our approach also captures long-term associations between tax policy and exposure to U.S. technological changes. Furthermore, the combination of including lagged values of the dependent variable, the use of fixed industry weights in the construction of theTechExposuremeasure, and year and country fixed effects should alleviate concerns regarding the most obvious potentially confounding factors. One concern is reverse causality, that is, that tax policy drives exposure to technological changes. As long as U.S. technological changes are not strongly affected by foreign country tax policies, the inclusion of lagged policy variables and the use of 1995 industry weights forTechExposureprevent this channel from confounding our results. As changes in tax policy cannot affect the industry weights, the only possible confounding channel has to go through U.S. technological changes. Another concern is that U.S. tax policy changes drive U.S. technological changes and, at the same time, affect the other countries’ tax policies (i.e., due to policy spillovers). This confounding channel should be blocked by the inclusion of lagged tax policy variables (Reinhart and Rogoff 2011).23A similar rationale holds for potentially confounding effects related to a country’s institutional and economic environment. Since such factors tend to be fairly constant over time, they should already be captured by our lagged variablesTaxPolicyi,[t−1:t−3]and country fixed effects. Finally, there might be some country-specific changes over time not captured by our controls, which could affect both tax policy and technological changes. However, these are again unrelated toTechExposurei,[t−1:t−3]by design because the industry weights are fixed over time and U.S. industry-level technological changes are unlikely to be related to such local changes. Two remaining issues potentially confounding our estimate of the relation between technological changes and tax policies (β1) are a potential correlation between U.S. technological change and global economic cycles and the possibility that U.S. technological changes affect not only other country’s tax policies via technological change spillovers but also competitive shocks. We address both points in additional analyses. Table 3presents descriptive statistics for our sample, which consists of 616 country-year observations for 34 countries. We lose some observations because of missing data on control variables and because we require several lags of technological changes.Table 3provides one important insight. Most countries do not use the full spectrum of anti–tax avoidance rules, as borne out by the 95th percentile ofAntiTAScorebeing 0.51. article.4. Results: 4.1. Main Results InTable 4, we report the results from estimating our main specification using anti–tax avoidance rules as the dependent variable. In column (1), we use our mainTechExposuremeasure, whereas we use the individual components ofTechExposurein column (2). The results suggest that exposure to U.S. technological changes are associated with tighter future anti–tax avoidance rules in a country, as indicated by the positive and significant coefficient forTechExposure[t−1:t−3]in column (1). When using the breakdown into the single components, we observe a positive association of changes in TFP with future anti–tax avoidance rules. One explanation for this finding is that greater TFP is often due to intangible assets, which facilitate tax avoidance, as we discuss inSection 2.2. Interpreting the economic magnitude is challenging due to the nature of the dependent variable as an index of rules and the main independent variable capturing exposure to U.S. technological change. Our results imply that an interquartile range increase inTechExposure, that is, an increase from the 25th to the 75th percentile, is associated with an increase in the anti–tax avoidance score of 0.044, or a 15% higherAntiTASc. A one-standard-deviation increase inTechExposureis associated with a 0.025 higher (or about 9% higher) anti–tax avoidance rule score. This suggests a sizeable increase in the strictness of anti–tax avoidance rules.24To put this into perspective, aTechExposurevalue of around one (i.e., close to a one-standard-deviation increase) occurs in country–years such as Germany in 2001, Italy in 2001, Finland in 2004, or Israel in 2010. Moreover, we frequently observe changes in the anti–tax avoidance score of the same magnitude as suggested by our previous estimates. For example, Germany increased its anti–tax avoidance score in 2001 by 0.025, Japan increased its score in 2001 by 0.056 (whereTechExposurewas 1.78), and Mexico increased its anti–tax avoidance score by 0.028 in 2005 (whereTechExposurewas 1.78). We consider this magnitude to be economically significant because a change by 0.028 inAntiTAScis about 10% of the sample average anti–tax avoidance score. One key potential concern about our main findings is that one single industry may drive our findings. Specifically, the Computer and Electronic Products industry exhibits the greatest technological changes in the United States. Hence, it is possible that this industry is responsible for our findings. In a robustness test, we thus individually drop single industries from theTechExposuremeasure. The resultingTechExposurecoefficients are reported inFigure 4. The results show that our main findings are robust to the exclusion of any single industry. As expected, excluding the Computer and Electronic Products industry has the largest, but still only a small, impact on the coefficient. The estimates remain very stable and significant irrespective of which industry is excluded. In columns (3) to (7) ofTable 4, we present additional robustness tests of the main association betweenTechExposureandAntiTASc. The objective of these tests is to demonstrate that some of our design choices do not drive the observed positive association of exposure to U.S. technological changes and antitax avoidance rules. First, we address the concern that the inclusion of prior tax policy outcomes could result in a bad control problem. The results in column (3) suggest that the exclusion of past tax policy outcomes does not alter the positive association between technological change and anti–tax avoidance rules (p= 6.1%). In the next step, we address concerns about the choice of industry weights from 1995. Columns (4) and (5) use either contemporaneous weights or weights lagged by one year (relative to the measure ofTechExposure). We continue to find support for a positive association of technological changes and anti–tax avoidance rules. In column (6), we address the concern that U.S. technological changes are correlated with global boom and bust cycles. Because countries might react to such cycles by changing their tax system, our findings might be confounded by the cycles. Although we try to account for this using year fixed effects and controlling for a country’s GDP growth, GDP per capital, or unemployment, there might still be unobservable variation. To further address this point, we add interactions of country dummy variables with lagged oil prices in column (4) as a measure of economic cycles (Bernanke et al. 1997). The idea is that (a) oil prices are highly related to the global economic situation and that (b) country-specific oil price exposure should account for heterogeneity in exposure to economic developments. Our main results are very similar when including interactions of country fixed effects with the oil price. Finally, we address a concern about the factor analysis underlying theTechExposuremeasure. We face a tradeoff in the choice of the time period. Although our approach is potentially subject to a look-ahead bias, using information only up to yeartmight lead to less precise estimates, especially for earlier years with only very few observations. To show that our results are robust to a rolling window construction ofTechExposure, column (7) uses only the time series of ΔTFP, ΔLabProd, and ΔCapIntup to timet. We continue to find a positive association betweenTechExposureand anti–tax avoidance rules, albeit with smaller coefficients (but similar statistical significance levels). This is unsurprising because using rolling windows leads to noisier estimates with only a few years to perform the factor analysis. 4.2. Is the Change in Anti–Tax Avoidance Rules Related to Reactive and Proactive Policymaking? In this section, we explore whether our approach capturesreactivetax policy (i.e., technological change increases profit shifting and policymakers respond to observed change in profit shifting by tightening anti–tax avoidance rules) or if our results also capture proactive tax policy, that is, that there is a positive association between technological change and anti–tax avoidance rules because policymakers anticipate potential increases in profit shifting and tax avoidance. Arguably, part of tax policy is reactive because governments observe certain actions by firms and then respond to this behavior. At the same time, there are very good arguments for why part of tax policy changes might relate to policymakers proactively responding to anticipated future outcomes. We approach this point in three steps, as follows: (1) discussing proactive tax policy with several examples, (2) empirically controlling for reactive tax policy, and (3) exploring differences in the long-term orientation of countries. 4.2.1. Discussion of Reactive vs. Proactive Tax Policy. Proactive tax policy is akin to the classification of tax policy being “endogenous” according toRomer and Romer (2010). That is, policymakers want to induce a certain behavior through tax policy. For example, policymakers endogenously (or, in other words, proactively) implement a tax cut to boost investments because they forecast a recession. Romer and Romer give the example of the U.S. Tax Reduction Act of 1975, where policymakers “were cutting taxes because the economy was predicted to fall further, and they were attempting to mitigate the decline” (Romer and Romer 2010, p. 769). Moreover, policymakers around the world refer directly to the future when arguing for general tax policy changes. For example, the 42nd president of the United States, Bill Clinton, said on May 17, 1998, that “[w]e must also ensure that our institutions and structures keep pace with the rapid technological and economic changes under way in the world.” One of the items in his action plan was to “[carry] out structural reforms, including making tax and benefit systems more employment friendly.”25In the same spirit, George W. Bush said on February 13, 2006, that “[i]f the United States expects to remain competitive in a global economy, we must encourage our private sector to continue to invest in leading-edge technologies, and therefore, we ought to make the research and development tax credit a permanent part of our Tax Code.”26Similar quotes can be found by policymakers in other countries.27 Likewise, there are many anecdotes where policymakers have changed anti–tax avoidance rules or implemented certain clauses in anticipation of potential future events. For example, the introduction of a flat tax on interest and dividend income of 25% in Germany in 2008 opened the door for tax minimization strategies by financing companies via shareholder loans instead of equity. German policymakers anticipated potential future tax avoidance strategies in the tax reform process and included many antiabuse clauses that shut down most of the tax avoidance opportunities related to shareholder loans. The policymaker even expanded these antiabuse clauses to cover sophisticated back-to-back financing schemes through banks. Similarly, in the early 1990s, the Swedish government introduced a dual income tax with relatively high personal income tax rates on wages, but lower tax rates on dividends. This provided potential incentives for individuals to channel income through a corporation to enjoy the lower tax rates. Policymakers anticipated this potential tax avoidance strategy and implemented (complicated) rules for closely held companies to determine maximum amounts of dividends subject to the lower dividend tax rate. Finland implemented comparable rules to prevent such income shifting right at the start when they changed the tax system. Although not being directly related to technological change, these anecdotes show that policymakers anticipate taxpayers’ behavior when setting anti–tax avoidance rules.28 4.2.2. Controlling for Reactive Tax Policy. Our baseline empirical approach captures reactive and proactive tax policy changes. To account for reactive tax policy, we add two controls for the tax avoidance behavior of companies headquartered in the same country. We calculate the median Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (GAAP) effective tax rate of firms headquartered in the same country (MedETRt−1:t−3; see alsoShevlin et al. (2019)using this country-level measure of tax avoidance). As an alternative control, we calculate a measure of profit shifting incentives, building onHuizinga and Laeven (2008). We define this measure, denotedForETRt−1:t−3, as the weighted average tax rate of all foreign countries that have FDI in this host country. We use bilateral FDI stocks as the weights.29With these measures, we control for the reactive part of tax avoidance as best as possible. The incremental association of technological change and anti–tax avoidance rules should then pick up the proactive part of tax policy changes. The results are reported inTable 5, columns (1) to (4). After controlling for the tax avoidance behavior of local companies and of multinationals usingMedETRandForETR, we continue to find a significant and positive association between exposure to U.S. technological change and anti–tax avoidance rules. The fact that the additional control variables are nonsignificant can potentially be explained by the fact that country fixed effects and year fixed effects already absorb a substantial part of the variation of interest. Exploring More Long-Term–Oriented vs. More Short-Term–Oriented Countries. In addition to the previous test, we also examine cross-sectional differences between countries whose cultures are categorized as more long-term versus more short-term oriented. The rationale is that if technological changes are associated with proactive policymaking, then we should see a stronger (or at least not a weaker) association for countries whose policymakers are more likely to be forward looking, that is, proactive. Because this is inherently hard to measure, we build a joint proxy based on three dimensions. First, countries whose cultures are more long-term oriented or avoid uncertainty are presumably more likely to act proactively. Second, we use (1) the Hofstede country characteristics classification for long-term orientation and (2) the uncertainty avoidance to proxy for both dimensions. Third, we use gross domestic savings rates from the World Bank. Greater domestic savings rates indicate more long-term thinking. For each of these measures, we calculate the tercile rank score and sum these tercile ranks to arrive at theLong-Term Score. We then use an indicator variable,High Long-Term, which is equal to one for countries with above-medianLong-Term Scorevalues and zero otherwise. We expect the interactions betweenTechExposureandHigh Long Termto be positive. Importantly, these interactions should not be negative, as that would suggest that the association between technological change and future tax policy is related to reactive tax policy. Columns (5) and (6) ofTable 5present the results. We measure the long-term score measure int −1 in column (5), whereas column (6) uses the respective average for each of the three measures over the sample period. Although the latter approach could introduce an endogeneity concern, it has the advantage of reduced sample attrition. In both cases, the coefficient onTechExposureremains positive and significant. Importantly, the interaction terms are positive and significant in both cases, suggesting that the association between exposure to U.S. technological changes and anti–tax avoidance rules is stronger for more long-term oriented, that is, more proactive, countries. We view this as tentative evidence that our mainTechExposurecoefficient inTable 4is also related to proactive tax policy changes and not solely related to reactive tax policy. 4.3. Cross-Sectional Variation in the Technological Change–Tax Policy Relation In this section, we assess the role of two potential channels that may explain the association between technological change and anti–tax avoidance rules. We first explore the role of the increased use of intangibles. Subsequently, we examine the role of increased capital mobility due to technological change.30 4.3.1. Assessing the Role of Intangibles. We start by exploring differences in the importance and role of intangibles. If, as we suggest, technological change works through intangible assets, then countries that are exposed to U.S. technological changeandto intangible assets from the United States should exhibit a stronger association betweenTechExposureand anti–tax avoidance rules. To test this notion, we construct a test to examine how the association betweenTechExposureand anti–tax avoidance rules relates to a country’s stock of acquired U.S. technological knowledge. We followVenturini (2015)in constructing a measure of the accumulated stock of technological knowledge. The idea is that a greater stock of ICT-related knowledge is associated with a higher likelihood of subsequent technological advancements through intangibles.31 We then split the sample into countries with high versus low relative stocks of acquired U.S. technological knowledge (denotedPct.US.Stock). We split the sample at the median value ofPct.US.Stock. The results are reported in columns (1) and (2) ofTable 6. TheTechExposurecoefficient is positive and highly significant in countries with high exposure to U.S. knowledge stock. In contrast, the association between technological change and anti–tax avoidance is nonsignificant if the exposure to U.S. know-how is small. The results are thus consistent with the idea that there is a stronger association between U.S. technological change exposure and anti–tax avoidance rules if a country is also exposed to U.S. technological know-how and intangibles. We also report thetstatistic for the statistical difference across partitions. Despite the very small sample size, we obtain atstatistic of 1.68 for the difference. We corroborate these findings using a measure of the incentives to shift profits using intangible assets. The idea is that if a country is exposed to high (low) income shifting incentives via intangible assets, the association between exposure to U.S. technological change and anti–tax avoidance rules should be strong (weak). To test this, we use the FDI-weighted average statutory tax rate fromSection 4.2to sort countries into cases where FDI-connected foreign countries have relatively low tax rates (i.e., high profit shifting incentives) and cases where connected countries have high tax rates (i.e., low profit shifting incentives). InTable 6, columns (3) and (4) provide separate regression results for countries where connected countries have low statutory tax rates and countries where connected countries have high statutory tax rates, respectively. In the subsample of countries with high profit shifting incentives, there is a significant and positive association betweenAntiTAScoreandTechExposure. In countries, where connected countries have relatively high tax rates, there is a small and only marginally significant association between exposure to U.S. technological change and tighter anti–tax avoidance rules. However, we note that the difference in coefficients is not significant at conventional levels presumably due to the small sample size of only 34 countries and thus a lack of statistical power. Keeping this limitation in mind, these results are consistent with the notion that the increased use of intangible assets (induced by technological changes) and the resulting increased profit shifting opportunities are associated with tighter anti–tax avoidance rules. 4.3.2. Assessing the Role of Capital Mobility. Increasing capital mobility can be another reason why technological change might be associated with anti–tax avoidance rules. Increased firm mobility increases the risk of firms leaving the country (“capital flight”). Because tighter anti–tax avoidance rules represent additional cost to firms, these rules are associated with lower investments (seeLi et al. (2021) and our own tests in Section A of the Online Appendix). Thus, by tightening anti–tax avoidance rules, countries may risk firms leaving the country. An alternative way to attract corporate capital or not risking that firms leave the country is refraining from tightening anti–tax avoidance rules relative to other countries’ efforts.32The attendant flexibility and increased opportunity to reduce corporate tax payments could serve as an indirect tax incentive for corporate investment in a country. To test this notion, we explore variation in agglomeration rents. Greater agglomeration rents are associated with a lower risk of capital flight for a country.Baldwin and Krugman (2004)argue that industrial concentration and well-developed infrastructure can result in agglomeration rents. For instance, a car manufacturer will likely locate its business close to suppliers. The resulting economies of scale and network effects from corporate agglomeration may allow countries with high industrial concentration to tighten anti–tax avoidance rules without losing capital owing to lock-in effects. To test for such cross-sectional variation, we use country size as a proxy for agglomeration rents and rerun our baseline regression separately for small and large countries. We use either the population size (columns (5) and (6)) or total GDP (columns (7) and (8)) to split our countries into small versus large. Table 6provides regression results separately for large and small countries. We provide the results for “large” countries in columns (5) and (7) and for “small” countries in columns (6) and (8). Although there is no association between exposure to technological change and anti–tax avoidance rules in smaller countries, large countries exhibit a positive and significant relation between exposure to technological changes and anti–tax avoidance rules. This is consistent with the idea that smaller countries use less strict anti–tax avoidance rules to attract corporate investments or to prevent losing mobile capital due. In contrast to small countries, large countries (e.g., owing to agglomeration rents) are less at risk for capital outflows and can protect tax revenues by tightening their anti–tax avoidance rules. To corroborate the role of capital mobility, we split the sample into countries with low versus high trade openness in columns (9) and (10). The idea is that countries with high trade openness have more elastic capital flows (Horst 1971), thereby being exposed to greater risk of capital flight when tax burdens increase. Such countries may thus shy away from tightening anti–tax avoidance rules out of fear of discouraging investments. In contrast, the risk of capital flight is smaller in countries that are less open. Consistent with this rationale, we find a significant and positive relation betweenAntiTAScoreandTechExposureonly for the subsample with low trade openness. Countries with high trade openness do not exhibit a significant association between technological change and subsequent tightening of anti–tax avoidance rules.33 4.4. Exposure to U.S. Technological Change and Other Tax Policy To account for the notion that policymakers may not only adjust anti–tax avoidance rules but also other tax rules,Table 7reports the results of estimating our main model using statutory tax rates (columns (1) and (2)) and investment incentives (columns (3) and (4)), respectively as dependent variables. We do not find any significant relation between exposure to U.S. technological change or any of its components and statutory tax rates. In contrast, we find a negative association between technological change and a country’s future investment incentives, as indicated by the negative and significant coefficient onTechExposurein column (3). The results in column (4) further suggest that only exposure to U.S. labor productivity (ΔLabProd) is negatively associated with capital investment incentives. This association is consistent with the notion that when labor becomes more productive, capital investment incentives appear to be less warranted to align economic realities with the tax system. In Panels B and C, we replicate the main robustness tests for these two other tax policy tools, obtaining very similar results as in Panel A. Validation Analyses: U.S. Technological Change and Outcomes in Other Countries In the final step, we validate the implicit assumption behind our findings that firms in a foreign industry with significant exposure to U.S. technological changes will incorporate those changes themselves and use those developments to more effectively minimize taxes and to become more productive. This validation test is important because it is ex ante equally plausible that U.S. technological changes represent a negative competitive shock: Although U.S. firms experience a productivity boost, same-industry firms in other OECD countries may be at a competitive disadvantage. We test our assumptions empirically. If U.S. firms receive a productivity boost and competitors in other countries are subsequently at a competitive disadvantage, then this should be reflected in two components of return on assets—that is, lower efficiency (measured by asset turnover) and pricing (measured by profit margins)—for those disadvantaged firms. Exposure to U.S. technological changes should generally help increase the efficiency of the assets used if U.S. technological changes have positive spillovers on firms in other countries. If this puts firms at a disadvantage, we might not see increases in asset turnover ratios in foreign countries. We examine the relation between changes in return on assets, asset turnover, and profit margins with exposure to U.S. technological changes using data from Compustat Global. By defining the dependent and the main independent variable in first differences, we examine the association of exposure to U.S. technologicalchangeswithchangesin the variable of interest. We include industry-year fixed effects as well as various firm-level and country-level controls. We also include country fixed effects to account for time-invariant country characteristics as in our country-level analysis. The results of this firm-level test are reported inTable 8. We find an insignificant association with return on assets (column (1)). This suggests that, on average, there is no lower profitability associated with U.S. technological changes. Importantly, we find a positive and significant relation between changes in asset turnover andTechExposure, which is consistent with the notion of a positive global productivity shock (see column (2)). In column (4) ofTable 8, we also examine the association of U.S. technological changes with tax planning outcomes. Consistent with the notion that exposure to U.S. technological changes may enable firms to more effectively minimize taxes (e.g., via the greater use of intangibles or greater capital mobility), we find that U.S. technological changes are associated with lower effective tax rates of firms in other countries. Overall, these findings are consistent with U.S. technological change (1) having positive spillovers on other countries and (2) enabling firms to minimize taxes. article.5. Conclusion: We use a sample of 34 OECD countries over the period 1996–2016 to examine the association of technological changes and corporate tax policy, particularly anti–tax avoidance rules. We observe stricter anti–tax avoidance rules over time but also more variation in these rules across countries despite harmonization efforts. Exploiting a new cross-country measure of exposure to U.S. technological changes in a shift-share design, we found a positive association between exposure to U.S. technological changes and tighter anti–tax avoidance rules. Larger countries seem to drive this association. We do not find this association for smaller countries, which are exposed to a greater risk of capital flight. Moreover, this association is driven by countries with greater exposure to U.S. know-how, countries facing profit shifting pressures, and countries that are less open with respect to their trade. Our results have two main implications. First, our results are consistent with smaller countries using looser anti–tax avoidance rules as a potential way to attract investments. Larger countries appear to place more weight on preserving corporate tax revenues, as indicated by the positive association of technological change and stricter anti–tax avoidance rules. Second, future empirical work exploiting changes in these rules should take into account their potential endogeneity with respect to technological changes. Moreover, it is also critical for managers to understand when and how policymakers adjust anti–tax avoidance rules because managers base strategic decisions on assumptions and expectations about forward-looking effective marginal tax rates (Scholes et al. 2014,Graham et al. 2017). We also acknowledge several limitations of our study. Our measure for exposure to U.S. technological changes could only measure such changes on a coarse level. Although rooted in economic theory, our approach might suffer from measurement error. Although we have no reason to suspect that this measurement error is correlated with a country’s tax policy, a more precise measure of technological changes would provide statistical tests with more power. Likewise, our anti–tax avoidance rule index is a summary measure with some arguably arbitrary choices, such as weighting each item similarly. Another potential limitation is that we could only examine the rules set by policymakers and not the level of enforcement of those rules. However, our objective was to examine the determinants of corporate tax policy choices. An interesting and important avenue for future research is the analysis of the consequences for management decision making of setting corporate tax policy along the three dimensions for which we collected data, that is, anti–tax avoidance rules but also tax rates and investment incentives. A final limitation is that our approach only identified associations but not a causal relation.
